title: Discrete distributions: Bernoulli, binomial, Poisson
intro: The normal distribution describes measured quantities — height, weight, time. But a huge share of an analyst's data is COUNTS: clicked or not, how many purchases, how many errors per hour. Count data has its own distributions. Let's cover the main three — and where each shows up. Press "pour": a real sample's histogram settles onto the theoretical PMF.

beats.text: The list at the top of the widget offers three discrete distributions. Start with the simplest yes/no experiment: clicked / did not, bought / did not, heads / tails. That is the Bernoulli distribution (selected by default): just two bars whose heights are p and 1−p. Drag p — the weight flows between "yes" and "no". This is the brick almost everything else is built from.

How to read the chart and the two buttons at the top right. Horizontal axis — the possible values (integers), vertical — probability. The PMF button (probability mass function) shows bars: each bar's height is the probability of getting EXACTLY that value, and the bars sum to 1. The CDF button (cumulative probability) shows something else: the height at a point is the probability of getting a value LESS THAN OR EQUAL to it; the line climbs in steps from 0 to 1. The yellow histogram (after "pour") is no longer theory but a real sample: as the draws grow it settles onto the theoretical PMF.
Pick "Binomial" from the list. Repeat a Bernoulli experiment n times and count the successes — that is the binomial distribution. How many of 10 emails get opened? How many of 100 visitors buy?
beats.predict: Set n = 10, p = 0.5. Where will the peak be — and what happens to the shape if you raise n to 20?
beats.reveal: The peak sits at n·p (with p = 0.5 and n = 10, around 5 successes). As n grows, the binomial looks more and more like a bell — an early hint of the central limit theorem. Conversion in an A/B test is exactly a binomial story: n visitors, each with chance p to buy.

beats.text: Now pick "Poisson". It describes the number of rare events per interval: calls to a call center per hour, server failures per day, orders per minute. It has one parameter λ — the average number of events per interval, which is simultaneously its variance.
beats.predict: Raise λ. What happens to the shape and where does the peak go?
beats.reveal: The peak moves right toward λ, and the shape turns from skewed (small λ) to nearly symmetric (large λ). Poisson is the workhorse for event streams: load, incoming tickets, defects per batch.

beats.text: How to choose? One yes/no experiment → Bernoulli. A fixed number of experiments, counting successes → binomial. A stream of rare events per interval of time/area → Poisson. All three are about COUNTS, so the values are integers, and the bars are probabilities of specific numbers (PMF), not a smooth density.

definitions.text: one experiment with two outcomes. The parameter is the success probability p.
the number of successes in n independent Bernoulli trials. Mean n·p.
the number of rare events per interval. Mean = variance = λ.
probability mass function (for discrete data): bar height = probability of exactly that integer value. All bars sum to 1.
the distribution function (cumulative probability): the value at x = the probability of a result ≤ x. Climbs from 0 to 1.

practice: For an analyst this is not an abstraction but a daily tool. Conversion (bought / did not) is Bernoulli per user and binomial per group; that is why confidence intervals for A/B conversion are computed under the binomial model.
Poisson describes load and streams: how many requests per second, how many support tickets per day, how many defects per batch. Knowing λ you can estimate the chance of overload and plan capacity.
realLife: Insurers and call centers plan resources with Poisson: the average number of events is known, and the distribution prices the "peak days".
Quality control counts defects per batch (Poisson/binomial) and decides when to sound the alarm. And any "share"-type metric is binomial at heart.
